title: Can radical skepticism ever be decisively answered?
description: Tests whether humans can justify certainty, or only workable confidence.

args: Some beliefs are self-justifying, like basic logic or immediate experience.
Best explanation arguments support common sense beliefs better than skepticism does.
Any justification can be questioned without circularity.
Certainty requires a foundation that humans cannot access.

title: Does teleportation preserve personal identity?
description: Tests whether being the same person depends on continuity or pattern.

args: What matters is psychological continuity: memories, values, intentions.
A perfect copy with the same mind continues the same person in practice.
Destruction and copying creates a new individual with borrowed memories.
Continuity of the original body or brain matters for being the same person.

title: Should people keep beliefs while no strong evidence disproves them?
description: Tests whether belief is default or must be earned.

args: Most everyday knowledge works because trust and default assumptions are practical.
Demanding proof for every belief leads to paralysis.
Default belief encourages superstition and misinformation.
The burden of proof should sit with the person making the claim.

title: Is online infidelity morally comparable to physical infidelity?
description: Tests whether betrayal is about action, intention, or harm.

args: Emotional betrayal and secrecy can harm trust the same way.
Intent and commitment violation matter more than physical contact.
Physical intimacy adds a distinct level of violation and risk.
Some online behavior can be fantasy without real-world betrayal.

title: Can committing a smaller wrong be morally justified to prevent a greater harm?
description: Tests whether outcomes can justify morally troubling actions.

args: Preventing large-scale harm can outweigh a smaller violation.
Moral rules exist to protect well-being, so exceptions can be rational.
Allowing exceptions creates a slippery path to abuse.
Some acts violate human dignity and should be forbidden regardless of outcome.

title: Does morality depend on God's commands?
description: Tests whether right and wrong come from divine authority or independent reasons.

args: God is the ultimate authority, so moral obligation requires divine grounding.
Objective morality fits better with a moral lawgiver than with human opinion.
If God commands it because it is good, goodness exists independently.
If goodness is only what God commands, morality can seem arbitrary.

title: Can free will exist if the future is predictable in principle?
description: Tests whether choice requires indeterminism or only internal control.

args: Freedom can mean acting from one's reasons without coercion.
Predictability does not remove authorship of decisions.
If actions are fixed in advance, alternatives are not genuinely possible.
Responsibility seems unfair if choices could never have been different.

title: Is the veil of ignorance the best method for designing a just society?
description: Tests whether fairness requires ignorance of personal advantage.

args: It reduces bias and forces rules that protect everyone.
It promotes fair institutions by removing self-interest from design.
Real justice needs concrete knowledge of history and inequality.
People behind the veil may still choose risk-averse rules that limit freedom.

title: If every part of a ship is replaced over time, is it still the same ship?
description: Tests identity through change, continuity, and definition.

args: Identity can track continuity of function, ownership, and history.
Gradual change preserves sameness better than sudden replacement.
If all original material is gone, the original object no longer exists.
'Same ship' becomes a naming convention, not a fact about identity.

title: Would immortality make a meaningful life harder to sustain?
description: Tests whether meaning depends on finitude.

args: Endless time can erode urgency and commitment.
Goals lose weight if they can always be postponed.
Meaning can come from relationships, creativity, and ongoing growth.
More time can allow deeper mastery and long-term projects.

title: Can harming one innocent person ever be justified to save many?
description: Tests the limits of utilitarian tradeoffs versus rights.

args: Extreme cases make outcome-based ethics compelling.
Refusing may cause preventable catastrophe and greater suffering.
Innocent people should not be used as tools.
Allowing it institutionalizes abuse and destroys trust.

title: Do people in wealthy countries have strong duties to sacrifice comfort to help distant strangers?
description: Tests whether distance reduces obligation.

args: Preventing serious harm matters regardless of geography.
Modest sacrifice can save lives, so it is morally required.
Special obligations to family and community can take priority.
Unlimited duty becomes unworkable and may reduce long-term help.

title: If someone could act with guaranteed anonymity, would morality mostly collapse?
description: Tests whether morality depends on enforcement or character.

args: Many people behave morally because of consequences and reputation.
Without enforcement, self-interest dominates.
Many people act from conscience, empathy, and internal principles.
Moral identity and relationships still matter even without external punishment.

title: Should human rights be grounded in personhood rather than species membership?
description: Tests what qualifies a being for rights.

args: Rights track capacities like consciousness, interests, and agency.
It explains why some nonhumans might deserve protections.
Human rights protect all humans equally, including those with limited capacities.
Personhood standards can exclude vulnerable humans and invite abuse.

title: Can something count as art without being intentionally created as art?
description: Tests whether art requires intention or can be discovered.

args: Context and interpretation can transform objects into art.
Found art and natural beauty can produce artistic experience.
Art requires an artist's intention and communicative act.
Without intention, it is aesthetic appreciation, not art.

title: If prediction were reliable, would pre-emptive punishment be justified?
description: Tests justice, prevention, and due process.

args: Preventing serious harm can outweigh punishing after the fact.
If prediction is nearly certain, it resembles evidence of intent.
Punishment requires an act, not a forecast.
Systems will be abused, and errors destroy legitimacy.

title: Does religious pluralism weaken rational religious commitment?
description: Tests how disagreement affects justified belief.

args: Many incompatible claims reduce confidence that any one is uniquely true.
Geography and culture predict belief, suggesting contingency.
Disagreement exists in science and politics, yet commitment can be rational.
Personal experience and tradition can justify commitment without certainty.

title: Is suffering still morally significant if it is later forgotten?
description: Tests whether harm depends on memory or moment.

args: Suffering is bad in itself, even if not remembered later.
Forgetting does not erase the fact that a conscious being experienced pain.
If it leaves no lasting effect, the total harm may be smaller.
Some moral weight comes from long-term trauma and consequences.

title: If a person's psychology splits into two successors, does identity survive?
description: Tests whether identity can branch.

sideA.position: Identity does not survive as one person
args: One identity cannot equal two distinct future persons.
Identity requires a unique continuation, not branching.

sideB.position: Identity can survive in a different sense
args: What matters is psychological continuity, which both successors share.
Identity might be a practical concept, not an absolute fact.

title: Should dream skepticism affect everyday belief and action?
description: Tests whether unsolved skepticism should change daily life.

args: Daily life relies on practical certainty, not philosophical certainty.
Skeptic scenarios do not improve decisions in ordinary contexts.
If knowledge foundations are shaky, confidence should drop.
It encourages humility and better standards of evidence.

title: Is it better to be the funniest person in the room or the smartest?
description: Tests whether social influence or raw reasoning matters more in real life.

args: Humor builds trust, lowers tension, and makes people more open to ideas.
Social influence often matters more than having the best argument.
Better judgment leads to better decisions over time.
Charm can win moments, but intelligence handles complex problems.

title: Would having a pause button for life make people less happy overall?
description: Tests whether convenience improves life or drains urgency from it.

args: Too much control can remove spontaneity and urgency.
People may overuse the pause and avoid discomfort instead of growing through it.
Extra time would reduce stress and prevent rushed mistakes.
People could rest, reflect, and handle life with more intention.

title: Should embarrassing stories about you expire after a certain number of years?
description: Tests whether social memory should have moral limits.

args: People should not be trapped forever by minor past mistakes.
Forgiveness becomes meaningless if social punishment never ends.
Shared memories are part of real relationships and social accountability.
Trying to set an expiry date on stories can feel artificial and dishonest.

title: Is stealing credit for a good idea worse than having no ideas at all?
description: Tests whether dishonesty is worse than incompetence.

args: Dishonesty damages trust and punishes the person who actually contributed.
Lack of ideas is a weakness, but theft is a deliberate wrong.

sideB.position: No, having no ideas can be worse
args: A person with no ideas may still block progress or depend fully on others.
In some settings, results matter more than who first had the idea.

title: Would a world with no awkwardness be a worse world?
description: Tests whether discomfort plays a useful role in human connection.

args: Awkwardness can create humility, humor, and memorable bonding moments.
Social friction helps people learn tact and self-awareness.
Less awkwardness would make relationships smoother and reduce unnecessary stress.
People could be honest without wasting energy on social discomfort.

title: Should you judge a person for the music they play on speaker in public?
description: Tests whether public taste reveals character or just bad manners.

args: Forcing strangers to hear your choices shows disregard for shared space.
Public behavior can reveal how much a person respects others.

sideB.position: No, that judgment goes too far
args: Annoying behavior does not necessarily reveal deep moral character.
People can be inconsiderate in one setting and decent overall.

title: Is being chronically late a moral flaw or just poor self-management?
description: Tests whether disrespect and disorganization should be morally separated.

args: Repeated lateness shows disrespect for other people's time.
Knowing the harm and doing it anyway makes it partly moral.
Some people struggle with planning, time estimation, or attention, not respect.
The problem can be real and harmful without being a deep character failure.

title: Would people be kinder if everyone had a visible battery level above their head?
description: Tests whether hidden struggle is a major cause of social harshness.

args: Visible exhaustion would make mood and mistakes easier to understand.
People are often harsher because they cannot see what others are carrying.
Some people would still prioritize convenience over empathy.
Information alone does not guarantee patience or kindness.

title: Is it more important to be interesting than to be reliable?
description: Tests whether memorable impact matters more than steady trust.

args: Interesting people expand perspectives and make life richer.
Reliability without depth can make relationships functional but forgettable.
Trust is the foundation of friendship, work, and love.
Interesting people can attract attention, but reliable people sustain real bonds.

title: Should minor lies that keep conversations smooth be socially accepted?
description: Tests whether politeness sometimes outweighs strict honesty.

args: Small lies can protect feelings and avoid pointless conflict.
Social life often depends on tact rather than total honesty.
Normalizing small lies weakens trust and blurs moral lines.
People deserve respectful honesty, not false comfort.
